data.title: Tylenol's reputation among Republicans bounces back after Trump's warnings
data.description:
Tylenol’s brand reputation has bounced back among Republicans after suffering a blow following President Trump’s warning last fall that pregnant women should not take the medication due to a scientifically unproven risk of autism, according to YouGov data.

Trump made the remark about acetaminophen, the active ingredient in Tylenol, during an event last September in the Oval Office with Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. and other senior health officials.

Experts swiftly pushed back on the claim, disputing that there was any evidence to support a causal link between the medication and autism development.

A few days later, Dr. Mehmet Oz, who leads the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, attempted to soften Trump’s warning by saying that pregnant women should consult with their doctors and use the medication if they develop a high fever.  

YouGov found that after Trump’s initial comment, the percentage of Republican adults who said they would consider buying Tylenol when in the market next dropped from 48.8 percent to 39.4 percent within five weeks.

Among Democrats, the trend moved in the opposite direction, with a 4.6‑point increase in those saying they would buy the drug within five weeks, bringing consideration to 54.2 percent by early November.

“Rather than a brief spike, the increase held across several weeks, pointing to a widening gap in how different political groups responded to the same set of events,” YouGov wrote.

There was less movement among the general public, moving from 48.2 percent on the day of Trump’s remarks to 45 percent by mid-December.

46 percent of all U.S. adults, including 50 percent of Democrats and 45.5 percent of Republicans, said they would consider purchasing Tylenol as of Jan. 19.

YouGov also tracked overall public perception of the brand, finding that its combined score — measuring impression, value, quality, satisfaction, and recommendation — fell to 31.8 among Republicans in early November before leveling off toward the end of 2025.

A more complex pattern emerged when looking specifically at parents, YouGov noted, with consideration and index scores moving in opposite directions.

Consideration among parents rose sharply from 43.5 percent in late September to a peak of 60.3 percent by mid-October. That trend did not hold, however, with brand consideration now returned almost exactly where it was before Trump’s comments.

The perception of Tylenol dropped to its lowest level of 22.8 by Jan. 6, and though it has rebounded slightly, it remains below its pre-September 2025 level.

“Taken together, the two metrics highlight how different aspects of brand health can move on different timelines: willingness to consider the brand increased in the immediate aftermath, while broader brand sentiment adjusted more gradually over time,” YouGov wrote.

YouGov calculated the metrics using data from its brand-tracking platform, which relies on daily surveys of U.S. adults.

data.title: Trump signs executive order on combating substance addiction
data.description:
President Trump signed an executive order aimed at combating drug addiction and substance abuse on Thursday, dubbing it “the Great American Recovery Initiative.” 

The initiative directs federal agencies to use grant funds to support addiction recovery and increase awareness surrounding addiction. The effort will also help agencies consolidate a number of programs including those pertaining to drug prevention, treatment and recovery support. 

“Now we’re taking a bold action to help Americans struggling with all forms of addiction so that they can get the help and the support they need so that they can free themselves from the horrible burden of dependency,” Trump said, speaking in the Oval Office. 

Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. and Kathryn Burgum, who is married to Interior Secretary Doug Burgum, joined Trump at the White House. Kathryn Burgum has been sober for 22 years. 

The signing also included Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services Administrator Mehmet Oz and the recently appointed White House drug czar, Sara Carter. Trump’s special envoy to the Middle East Steve Witkoff, whose son died of a drug overdose, was also present, telling of his son’s story.

Trump has opened up in the past about his family’s connection to addiction. His brother, Fred Trump, died at the age of 42 because of a heart attack linked to alcohol addiction. 

“Many of those with me today have personally known the heartache of a loved one taken by drug or alcohol addiction. I do,” President Trump said.

During his first administration in 2019, Trump declared a commitment to ending the opioid crisis and spoke about his late brother. 

“I don’t know that I’d be working, devoting the kind of time and energy and even the money we are allocating to [the effort],” Trump told The Washington Post in an interview in 2019. “I don’t know that I’d be doing that had I not had the experience with Fred.”

data.title: Progressive outlet The Nation nominating city of Minneapolis for Nobel Peace Prize
data.description:
Progressive outlet The Nation is nominating the city of Minneapolis for the Nobel Peace Prize in the wake of tensions over President Trump’s immigration agenda in the city.

In a Wednesday piece, The Nation’s editors stated that they “are in the process of formally nominating the city of Minneapolis and its people for” this year’s prize. The piece also includes a nomination statement addressed to The Norwegian Nobel Committee and set to be sent Friday.

“Through countless acts of courage and solidarity, the people of Minneapolis have challenged the culture of fear, hate, and brutality that has gripped the United States and too many other countries. Their nonviolent resistance has captured the imagination of the nation and the world. Renee Good’s widow has said, ‘They have guns; we have whistles,’” the statement reads.

“Those whistles alert the residents of Minneapolis when they are threatened. But they have done more than that. They have awakened Americans to the threat of violence that extends from governments that unjustly and irresponsibly target their own people,” the statement continues.

In the last few weeks, Minneapolis has been rocked by tensions over President Trump’s strict immigration agenda. The city has witnessed two fatal shootings by federal immigration authorities this month, with Alex Pretti and Renee Good’s deaths sparking widespread anger.

On Thursday, White House border czar Tom Homan publicly noted internal changes for Minnesota-based federal immigration operations

“President Trump wants this fixed, and I’m going to fix it, with your help,” Homan said at a press conference.

The press conference included Homan’s first public remarks after he was sent to Minnesota on Monday.

“The people of Minneapolis and their elected leaders have demonstrated an extraordinary and sustained commitment to human dignity and to the protection of vulnerable communities,” The Nation’s editors wrote in their nomination statement.

data.title: Minnesota Democrat on Homan de-escalation remarks: 'More conciliatory words, but no detail'
data.description:
Sen. Tina Smith (D-Minn.) said border czar Tom Homan’s press conference in Minnesota on Thursday morning did not provide the information she was looking for, including when federal immigration authorities will leave the North Star State.

“It was more conciliatory words, but no detail. And I’m looking for information about when troops are leaving,” Smith told CNN’s Manu Raju.

“I think ICE needs to get out of Minnesota. I think it is dangerous when they are there. So I’m looking for much more detail than he offered today,” she added.

Smith, alongside her Democratic counterpart, Sen. Amy Klobuchar (Minn.), has urged the Trump administration to end operations led by Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) in Minnesota after two U.S. citizens were killed by federal authorities this month. 

Homan on Thursday morning said Trump wants to “fix” immigration operations in Minnesota.

“I do not want to hear that everything that has been done here has been perfect. Nothing is ever perfect. Anything can be improved on. What we’ve been working on is making this operation safer, more efficient, by the book,” Homan said.

“The mission is going to improve because of changes we’re making internally. No agency organization is perfect. President Trump and I, along with others in the administration, have recognized that certain improvements could and should be made,” he added.

Protests against ICE ramped up after Alex Pretti was fatally shot by a Border Patrol agent this weekend, just days after Renee Good was shot by an ICE officer.

The size of the federal force in Minnesota has also ballooned. On Sunday, Klobuchar said ICE officers “outnumber” local police in Minneapolis and St. Paul, Minn. 3 to 1.

The Minnesota Democrat said “it’s more ICE agents and border control than our 10 metro police departments,” during an appearance on ABC’s “This Week.” 

“They are overwhelmed by this federal presence, as are our citizens, grabbing 2-year-olds away from their parents, putting them on planes to Texas and then my office has to go and get them back, or taking an elderly man in his underwear and then realizing they have the wrong guy,” she added.

ICE’s detention of a 5-year-old boy in Minnesota last week has also drawn particular criticism.

“This is a time where we should all be outraged,” Rep. Angie Craig (D-Minn) said in a video posted to X. “If this doesn’t pierce through your humanity, as a Republican member of Congress, if you can’t speak out about this, then you’ve got no humanity left.”

Senate Democrats are refusing to fund the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), pushing the government to the brink of a shutdown ahead of Friday’s deadline. Republicans agreed to split off DHS funding from a minibus funding other government agencies as part of deal struck Thursday.

data.title: McCarthy: 'You're gonna have a short shutdown,' but 'no one would really feel that'
data.description:
Former House Speaker Kevin McCarthy (R) said there will be a “short shutdown” after a major funding package failed to advance in the Senate on Thursday. 

Democratic senators blocked the funding package, demanding that funds included for the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) be stripped. Democrats say they cannot support funding for DHS amid the agency’s massive immigration crackdown in Minnesota, during which federal officers have shot and killed two people. 

Eight Republicans joined all Democrats in voting against the package. If the bill doesn’t pass by its Friday evening deadline, the government will partially shut down. Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) switched his vote to “no” for procedural reasons.

McCarthy said Democrats have a “little advantage” in these negotiations due to the deadline constraints and the party’s “united” front. 

Following the second fatal shooting in Minneapolis, Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) called for the DHS funding bill to be taken out of the funding package, which consists of six total bills. 

The Trump administration has been in talks with Schumer about potentially splitting off the DHS bill and advancing a vote on the five other bills included in the funding package. No agreement has been reached yet in the ongoing discussions, but a source with knowledge of the situation told The Hill on Thursday that discussions were moving in the Democrats’ direction. 

“If they get those five out, they fund 96 percent of the government going forward and you have a short shutdown, no one would really feel that because people would already be paid, and I think a calmer head can prevail,” McCarthy said.

Democratic Sen. Richard Blumenthal (Conn.) echoed this perspective on Thursday, telling reporters, “If there is one, I would expect that it would be very short.”

McCarthy credited President Trump for his work in his administration’s discussions with Schumer’s team.

“He has been negotiating the White House with the Senate Democrats,” McCarthy said. “I don’t think they really want to get a deal, but I think you’re going to see President Trump, this is where his negotiations will come into effect. There’s no telling — maybe there’s a last-minute deal — but I think the Democrats will hold up Homeland for a little while longer.”

data.title: Grid reliability projected to decline as data centers drive demand, watchdog says
data.description:
The outlook for electric grid reliability in North America is worsening, as demand may outpace supply in the years ahead, according to a key watchdog.

In its annual Long-Term Reliability Assessment, the North American Electric Reliability Corporation (NERC) warned that demand from new data centers and other large loads is expected to outpace energy supply growth.

The international regulatory authority significantly ratcheted up its 10-year summer peak demand forecast, expecting demand growth to be 69 percent higher than what it projected in last year’s report. 

“New data centers for artificial intelligence and the digital economy account for most of the projected increase in North American electricity demand over the next 10 years,” NERC’s report said.

The report indicated several parts of the country will be at “high risk” for electricity supply shortfalls by the end of the decade.

It said that states served by the Midcontinent Independent System Operator could be at high risk starting in 2028, while parts of Texas, the northwestern U.S., the mid-Atlantic and parts of the Midwest could be at high risk starting in 2029.

The report also warned that most new power set to come online includes solar and battery storage, which the report described as “inverter-based and weather-dependent resources that increase the complexity of planning and operating a reliable grid.”

It also noted that fossil fuel plants may retire in the next five years, “reducing the amount of generation that has fuel on site and impacting the system’s ability to respond to spikes in demand.”

NERC is a regulatory authority responsible for the continental U.S., Canada and the northern part of Baja California, Mexico. It was founded by representatives of the electric utility industry and was later designated by the U.S. government as the Electric Reliability Organization that develops and enforces standards for the grid.

“This assessment is not a prediction of failure but an early warning on the trajectory of risk,” said John Moura, NERC’s director of Reliability Assessment and Performance Analysis, in a statement. “The path forward is still manageable but only if planned resources come online and on time.”

To address the issues, the report recommends expediting new power sources and approval processes, among other strategies.

The Trump administration has broadly embraced data centers and has sought to fast-track their buildout — though recent messaging from the administration has also said they should bring their own power sources online.

The administration has been supportive of fossil fuels and has sought to hamper renewable energy projects.

data.title: Buttigieg endorses Klobuchar's Minnesota governor bid after they clashed in 2020 Dem primary
data.description:
Sen. Amy Klobuchar’s (D-Minn.) former Democratic rival, Pete Buttigieg, has backed her bid to be Minnesota’s next governor. 

The former Transportation secretary and former mayor of South Bend, Indiana, said he is “thrilled to support” Klobuchar following the senator’s campaign launch on Thursday.

“I’ve worked alongside her and seen firsthand how she shows up, listens, and takes action to get results for the people she serves,” Buttigieg posted on the social platform X on Thursday. “Minnesotans deserve steady, caring leadership now more than ever – and they will be very well served with Amy leading their state.”

Buttigieg’s endorsement is a notable change of tone from the 2020 presidential campaign trail, during which the Midwestern politicians notably clashed on several points. 

The acrimony between the pair culminated at the Democratic primary debate in February 2020 when Buttigieg slammed Klobuchar over forgetting the name of the Mexican president in an interview with Telemundo. The Minnesota legislator responded, “Are you trying to say that I’m dumb? Or are you mocking me here, Pete?”

Klobuchar needled the former mayor over his lack of experience in Washington, D.C., saying, “I am the one, not you, that has won statewide, in congressional district after congressional district.”

Buttigieg responded by questioning Klobuchar for her voting record in Congress, including her decision to approve the nomination of former Customs and Border Protection acting chief, Kevin McAleenan, who was tasked with carrying out the first Trump administration’s hardline immigration agenda. 

“If you’re going to run on your record in Washington, then you’ve got to own those votes, especially when it comes to immigration,” Buttigieg said. 

At the debate’s conclusion, Klobuchar rebuffed a customary handshake from him, signaling a split between the two moderate Democrats. 

On Thursday, Buttigieg seemed to put the bad blood behind him, calling Klobuchar, “my friend,” in his post endorsing her run.

If elected, Klobuchar would be Minnesota’s first female governor. The state’s primary will be held on Aug. 11.

data.title: Shapiro suggests 'strong case' to be made in Minnesota for voluntary manslaughter, obstruction
data.description:
Pennsylvania Gov. Josh Shapiro (D) said Thursday there is a “very strong case” to charge the Customs and Border Protection (CBP) official who shot Alex Pretti multiple times on Saturday with voluntary manslaughter. 

“I see a case that requires a state investigation and requires a state determination as to whether prosecution is warranted,” Shapiro said at an event hosted by the Christian Science Monitor. 

“I would argue, based on the publicly available evidence, that you could make a very strong case for voluntary manslaughter against the federal official who pulled the trigger multiple times.”

Video of the incident shows Pretti recording immigration officials before attempting to assist a woman whom one agent had pushed to the ground. At that point, he was shoved down himself and surrounded by officers, with one appearing to disarm him before another fired multiple rounds at the 37-year-old.

CBP, which oversees Border Patrol, notified Congress on Tuesday that two agents fired shots during the incident, according to the Associated Press.

Shapiro, who previously served as the Keystone State’s attorney general, also said there is a “very strong case” the Border Patrol agents committed obstruction of justice when they did not secure the crime scene.

“I think you might be able to make out a case for conspiracy, if there was a clear directive that that crime scene should be compromised, that you should not participate in an investigation,” the Pennsylvania governor added. “So I think all three of those charges could be warranted.”

Shapiro noted, though, that he is “not privy” to all the evidence that Minnesota Attorney General Keith Ellison (D) and local prosecutors have regarding the shooting. 

The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) took over the investigation into the incident over the objections of Drew Evans, the superintendent of the Minnesota Bureau of Criminal Apprehension (BCA), which oversees use-of-force incidents by officers in the state. 

In a suit brought by the BCA, a federal judge in Minnesota blocked DHS on Saturday from “destroying or altering” evidence from its probe. 

White House border czar Tom Homan, meanwhile, said at a Thursday press conference that the federal immigration enforcement operation in Minnesota has not been “perfect.”

“I do not want to hear that everything that has been done here has been perfect. Nothing is ever perfect. Anything can be improved on and what we’ve been working on is making this operation safer, more efficient, by the book,” said Homan, who President Trump dispatched to the state to handle the operation earlier this week.

data.title: Live updates: Senate vote pushes government closer to shutdown; Noem mum at Trump Cabinet meeting
data.description:
President Trump assembled his Cabinet for a meeting Thursday, touting his administration’s achievements after a year in office. He made some news in saying he would announce his nominee for Federal Reserve chair next week.

But in a break from tradition, not every Cabinet member spoke. Among those sitting and listening was Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, who has drawn immense scrutiny after two deaths at the hands of federal immigration officials in recent weeks.

On Thursday afternoon, Trump is slated to sign an executive order to coordinate a federal plan to combat drug addiction and substance abuse.

Meanwhile, on Capitol Hill, Senate Democrats and a few Republicans took down a procedural vote that would have advanced a major government funding package. Here’s why seven Republicans voted agaianst it. If a deal isn’t made by 11:59 p.m. Friday, part of the government will shut down.

Trump administration border czar Tom Homan said at a press conference Thursday morning that the immigration crackdown in Minneapolis will be done “by the book” as Trump looks to “fix” it.

“I do not want to hear that everything that has been done here has been perfect. Nothing is ever perfect. Anything can be improved on. What we’ve been working on is making this operation safer, more efficient, by the book,” Homan said Thursday.

Trump later Thursday will join first lady Melania Trump at the premiere of her documentary at the Kennedy Center.

Follow live below.

data.title: Shapiro says state has 'ratcheted up' preparation if federal immigration crackdown comes to Pennsylvania
data.description:
Pennsylvania Gov. Josh Shapiro (D) said Thursday that his administration is “preparing” should the Commonwealth become the next target of President Trump’s immigration crackdown amid growing concerns about clashes between residents and federal law enforcement officers across the country.

“I’m not going to get into specifics of what we’re doing … But I can tell you that we have ratcheted up our preparation should this come, and that is going to involve not just law enforcement response but working closely with the community,” Shapiro said during an event hosted by The Christian Science Monitor in Washington, D.C.

“And so I want the community to know where I stand. I hear them, and I want them to know that we’re gonna be in partnership together to make sure that they are safe and protected in our country,” Shapiro added.

Shapiro declined to go into further detail, noting only that his government was “prepared on every level.” He did, however, acknowledge there may be some limitations to his ability to respond should Trump decide to move in.

“If the President of the United States seeks to impose his will and the federal will on the Commonwealth, there may be some things we can’t stop,” Shapiro said, adding that he had taken lessons from previous federal deployments in California, Illinois and Minnesota.

“We have the benefit of hindsight there, and we’ve been preparing, and I think we have some things that we believe would be deployed to keep our citizens safe,” he continued.

Shapiro, who is running for reelection and widely seen as a possible 2028 presidential contender, railed against the Trump administration’s handling of immigration enforcement operations in Minnesota.

Protests have erupted across the Twin Cities and elsewhere over the fatal shootings of two U.S. citizens, Alex Pretti and Renee Good, by federal law enforcement officers this month during Operation Metro Surge.

“At the end of the day, the job of any executive is to keep their citizens safe: mayor, governor, president. It’s one of the reasons why I’m so troubled by what this president is doing. I mean, he claims to be the law-and-order president. He is literally undermining the safety and security of the people of Minnesota through these actions,” Shapiro said.

Trump has called to de-escalate the situation in Minneapolis as the White House tries to contain the political fallout from Pretti’s death, pulling some federal agents out of the state this week. He has not indicated where they could be headed next.
